word_list.pronunciation: hˈoʊld ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɪn kəntˈɛmpt

senses.definition: to have a strong feeling of disrespect or disgust toward someone or something

examples.x: The public held the company in contempt for their unethical practices.
She held her former business partner in contempt for betraying their trust.
He held the politician in contempt for their dishonesty and broken promises.
Many people hold corrupt officials in contempt for their abuse of power.

senses.definition: to occupy an official or formal position in a government, organization, or institution, typically for a specific term or period

examples.x: He was elected to hold office as the mayor of the city.
He has held office as the chairman of the board for over a decade.
The president will hold office for a four-year term.
She is the first woman to hold office as the CEO of this company.
Many people aspire to hold office as members of parliament.

senses.definition: to ensure that an individual takes responsibility for their actions and faces consequences for their behavior or performance

examples.x: The legal system is designed to hold individuals accountable for their actions.
When it comes to environmental issues, it's essential to hold corporations accountable for their impact.

senses.definition: to keep someone in captivity against their will as a result of what they did

examples.x: During the war, many soldiers were held prisoner by the enemy forces.

senses.definition: to keep someone as a captive with the intention of making demands or taking advantage

examples.x: Negotiators worked to secure the release of the journalists held hostage in a war zone.
Authorities are trained to handle situations where individuals are held hostage to minimize harm and secure their release.
The criminals planned to take hostage the employees of the bank during the robbery.
Terrorists stormed the embassy and threatened to take hostage the diplomats inside.
The escaped convict attempted to take hostage a family he encountered while fleeing from the authorities.

senses.definition: to link hands with someone as an expression of affection, unity, or support

examples.x: The couple walked along the beach, happily holding hands.
Friends often hold hands when they cross the street to ensure safety.
During the team-building exercise, participants were asked to hold hands to form a circle.
As a sign of solidarity, the protesters marched through the streets while holding hands.
Grandparents and grandchildren enjoy holding hands as they stroll in the park.

senses.definition: to keep someone confined or restrained, limiting their freedom of movement or action

examples.x: The rebel forces successfully liberated the townspeople held captive by the oppressive regime.
The explorers were held captive by a remote tribe deep in the jungle.
The army was able to take captive several enemy soldiers during the skirmish.
The pirates threatened to take captive the crew of the cargo ship unless their demands were met.

senses.definition: to maintain someone's focus, interest, or engagement
to have a negative opinion about someone because of their actions in the past
to have the potential for future success or positive outcomes

examples.x: The new medical treatment holds promise for patients with rare diseases.
The innovative technology holds promise for solving some of the world's most pressing environmental challenges.
The student's early achievements in mathematics hold promise for a future career in STEM fields.
The experimental drug holds promise as a potential treatment for Alzheimer's disease.
The discovery of ancient artifacts in the region holds promise for uncovering new insights into human history.

senses.definition: to remain valid or accurate over time or in various situations

examples.x: The fundamental principles of democracy continue to hold true, despite evolving social and political landscapes.
In science, the laws of physics hold true across different contexts and environments.
The lessons learned from history often hold true in understanding contemporary events and conflicts.
The values of honesty and integrity hold true in both personal and professional relationships.
The notion that 'practice makes perfect' holds true in mastering any skill or craft.

senses.definition: to regard something with great respect, honor, or devotion
to have a great amount of respect, admiration, or esteem for someone or something

examples.x: She is held in high regard by her colleagues for her integrity and dedication.
The professor is held in high regard by her students for her engaging teaching style.
The author is held in high regard by literary critics.
